name: Cap'n Proto / CapTP
summary: Cap'n Proto (Kenton Varda, 2013+) is a typed binary serialisation format with zero-copy reads and a distributed object-capability RPC protocol descended from E's CapTP. Promise pipelining lets dependent calls travel in one round-trip; three-vat introduction handles secure capability handoff. Production at Cloudflare Workers; OCapN under standardisation; Cap'n Web (2025) is the JavaScript-native sibling.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Cap'n Proto** (Kenton Varda, 2013+) is a typed binary serialisation format with zero-copy reads and a distributed object-capability RPC protocol descended from E's **CapTP** (Capability Transport Protocol). It is one of the few production-grade systems carrying the full ocap discipline — including unforgeable distributed capabilities and promise pipelining — into mainstream tooling.

The schema and the RPC are deliberately co-designed: interface refs are first-class wire types — a method can return a capability, accept a capability as a parameter, or take a capability that the caller introduces from a third vat. The combination of a typed IDL with first-class capability references is what makes Cap'n Proto the canonical example of capability discipline at the wire level.

sections.title: Schema language and zero-copy reads
sections.content:
Cap'n Proto's schema language (`.capnp` files) is a typed IDL similar in role to Protobuf's `.proto` and WIT files, but with two significant design differences:

- **Zero-copy reads**: serialised messages have a fixed offset layout that matches in-memory representation, so reading a field is a pointer dereference rather than a parse. The wire format *is* the in-memory format. There is no separate "deserialise to objects" step. Encoding is correspondingly designed for word-aligned random access.
- **Interface types are first-class**: Protobuf has only data types; Cap'n Proto's `interface` declares a remote object with methods, and references to such interfaces serialise as capability pointers. This is what closes the gap with WASI Component Model resource handles — both are typed binary IDLs where capability references are part of the schema.

The zero-copy property is also why Cap'n Proto is competitive with Flatbuffers for hot-path serialisation; the ocap and zero-copy stories reinforce each other rather than trading off.

sections.title: CapTP — promise pipelining and three-vat introduction
sections.content:
**Promise pipelining**: pre-send calls against the *result* of an in-flight call, eliminating chatty RPC patterns; equivalent to E's eventual-send composition. A client can write `db.lookup(id).getName()` and the second call travels with the first — the server-side composes them, returning only the final result. Round-trip count drops from N to 1 for chained dependent calls.

**Three-vat introduction**: secure handoff of a capability from vat A to vat C via vat B without B gaining authority over the introduced capability. This is the network-protocol equivalent of E's Granovetter operator — "introduction is the only way to grow the reference graph" — and it is the protocol-level guarantee that distributed ocap retains the ocap invariants of single-machine ocap.

**Distributed acyclic GC** (Spritely Goblins, Agoric): cross-vat ref counting with the ocap invariant preserved. The full distributed-GC story for ocap-cycle handling is still active research; current production deployments rely on acyclic graphs plus best-effort cycle detection at vat boundaries.

sections.title: Production, OCapN, Cap'n Web
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04):

- **Cap'n Proto** is in production at Cloudflare Workers (the inter-isolate communication substrate for Workers and Durable Objects), at Sandstorm.io (sandboxed-app personal cloud), and as the IPC layer for several other systems. The C++ reference implementation is mature; ports to Rust (`capnp-rust`), Go, JavaScript, and Python exist.
- **OCapN** (Spritely + Agoric) is in active standardisation — a vendor-neutral wire format for object-capability networking that aims to be the cross-implementation `IP for ocap`. Spritely Goblins, Agoric, and an experimental MetaMask LavaMoat backend all participate.
- **Cap'n Web** (Cloudflare 2025) is the JavaScript-native sibling of the same protocol family — a TypeScript implementation with the same promise-pipelining and capability semantics, runnable in browsers, Cloudflare Workers, Node.js, Bun, and Deno. Under 10 kB gzipped. The lesson generalises: **object-capability RPC is now economically deployable in browser-and-server JavaScript**, not just in research languages or kernel-class systems.
